skill.name: simplify
skill.description: Simplify and refine recently modified code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability without changing behavior.
skill.summary: Refines recently changed code so it is clearer, more consistent, and easier to maintain.
skill.author: brianlovin

security_audit.summary: The static findings were evaluated against the single SKILL.md file. Most hits are Markdown wording false positives, but the hardcoded http://CLAUDE.md reference and broad autonomous-editing language create minor publication risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded HTTP Reference in Instructions
low_findings.description: Line 12 tells the assistant to follow standards from http://CLAUDE.md. This is not executable network code, but the HTTP URL could lead an agent to attempt external retrieval instead of using a local project file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URL is directly present on line 12. Risk is limited because it appears in prose, not code that automatically performs a request.

low_findings.title: Broad Autonomous Editing Instruction
low_findings.description: Line 51 instructs the assistant to operate autonomously and proactively after code is written or modified. This can cause unexpected edits if a user expects explicit approval before cleanup changes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and directly affects agent behavior. It is a workflow safety concern, not evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: False Positive: External Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static hit at line 15 is inline Markdown text that says to prefer the function keyword. No shell backtick execution or command invocation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains Markdown inline code around a JavaScript keyword. There is no command, interpreter call, or user input execution path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: The static hits at lines 3 and 27 do not describe cryptographic operations. They are ordinary prose about simplifying code and removing unnecessary comments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are natural language instructions. No hashing, encryption, or algorithm selection appears in the file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The static hits at lines 18, 28, and 31 are prose about error handling, nested ternaries, and avoiding over-simplification. They do not request host, process, user, or network enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are coding-style guidance. No commands or instructions for environment discovery are present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Python HTTP Library Use
low_findings.description: The static hit at line 51 does not reference Python HTTP libraries. The line contains general instructions about autonomous code refinement.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains no import, library name, URL fetch, or code. It is prose and should not be treated as network implementation evidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded HTTP Reference
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes an HTTP URL in instructions. It should prefer a local CLAUDE.md reference or explicitly avoid external network retrieval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL is visible in the skill text. The risk depends on whether the host agent follows the URL automatically.

content.value_statement: Recently changed code can become harder to read after fast iteration. This skill reviews touched code and refines structure, naming, and consistency while preserving behavior.
actual_capabilities: Reviews recently modified code for readability and maintainability issues.
Simplifies complex control flow, nesting, and redundant abstractions.
Improves variable, function, and component naming for clarity.
Applies project-specific coding standards when they are available.
Preserves existing functionality while making code easier to understand.
Documents only significant changes that affect understanding.
limitations: It depends on visible recent changes and may miss untouched code paths.
It does not prove behavioral equivalence without tests or review.
Project standards must be available in context to be applied accurately.
It focuses on clarity, not performance tuning or architecture redesign.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Feature Work
use_cases.description: Refine a new feature after implementation so the final diff is easier to review.

use_cases.title: Prepare Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Reduce unnecessary complexity before opening a pull request for team review.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Code
use_cases.description: Apply local style rules to recently touched files during regular maintenance.

prompt_templates.title: Simplify My Last Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the simplify skill on my recently modified code. Preserve behavior and focus on readability.

prompt_templates.title: Refine for Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare a pull request for human review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the touched files and simplify code that is harder than necessary to understand. Keep the diff focused.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Project Standards
prompt_templates.scenario: Align changed code with repository conventions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Simplify my recent changes and apply the repository coding standards that are already present in this workspace.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Behavior-Preserving Cleanup
prompt_templates.prompt: Refine the current diff for clarity, remove redundant abstractions, avoid clever rewrites, and explain any meaningful behavior-preserving changes.

output_examples.input: A recently edited React component has repeated condition checks and unclear prop naming.
output_examples.output: The component is rewritten with clearer names, simpler branches, and the same rendered behavior.

output_examples.input: A utility function contains nested conditional logic and comments that repeat the code.
output_examples.output: The logic is split into clearer branches, obvious comments are removed, and edge cases remain unchanged.

output_examples.input: A pull request includes several small edits that do not follow local style conventions.
output_examples.output: The touched code is normalized to match project patterns without broad unrelated refactoring.

best_practices: Run tests after simplification to confirm behavior stayed the same.
Provide project standards or style files in context before using the skill.
Use it on a focused diff so changes remain easy to review.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to redesign large systems in one pass.
Do not accept simplifications that remove useful abstractions.
Do not rely on it as a substitute for tests or human review.

faq.question: Does this skill change program behavior?
faq.answer: It is designed to preserve behavior and only improve code clarity, consistency, and maintainability.

faq.question: Which files does it review?
faq.answer: It focuses on code recently modified or touched in the current session.

faq.question: Can it apply my repository standards?
faq.answer: Yes, when those standards are available in the workspace or conversation context.

faq.question: Is it only for JavaScript or TypeScript?
faq.answer: The instructions mention React and ES modules, but the core simplification guidance can apply to other code.

faq.question: Should I run tests after using it?
faq.answer: Yes. Tests are the best way to confirm that behavior was preserved.

faq.question: Does it perform security scanning?
faq.answer: No. It improves readability and maintainability, but it is not a dedicated security audit tool.
